nativeRange.habitat: Sandy and rubble substrates in lagoons, seagrass beds, and backreef zones of shallow tropical waters; grows upright from a buried sand-grower holdfast. Also found on fore-reef slopes and among coral rubble. Common in Florida, Caribbean, and Bahamian reef systems.

decorPreferences: open display area with strong overhead lighting
stable placement sheltered from direct powerhead flow

avoidWith: urchins that may dislodge or consume calcified segments
large herbivorous fish such as tangs that may graze heavily in smaller tanks

imageCandidates.notes: Primary product image from Mosaic Macros listing for Forked Halimeda (Halimeda incrassata). Shows characteristic upright forked branching with calcified three-lobed segments. Sole hobby-trade source for this specialty entry.

summary: A heavily calcified calcareous green macroalgae (family Halimedaceae) with distinctive forked, three-lobed segments growing upright from a sand-buried holdfast. Common in Caribbean reef systems; generally considered unpalatable to most fish. Requires stable calcium, magnesium, and high lighting.
careNotes:
Forked Halimeda (Halimeda incrassata) is a heavily calcified green macroalga in the family Halimedaceae, order Bryopsidales, class Ulvophyceae. It is distinguished from other Halimeda species by its characteristic three-lobed (trilobate) or forked segments, which give rise to its common trade name. The thallus grows upright from the substrate via a sand-grower holdfast that buries into soft substrates — a sand bed of at least 5 cm is generally advised to allow this holdfast to anchor. In the wild, H. incrassata is among the most common and ecologically significant Halimeda species in Caribbean reef systems, where it is a major contributor to shallow-water sediment production.

CALCIUM AND ALKALINITY: As a calcareous alga, H. incrassata deposits substantial calcium carbonate (aragonite, approximately 60–80% of dry mass) into its segments. In a healthy, actively growing specimen, this calcification process measurably consumes calcium and alkalinity from the water column. Maintaining standard reef calcium levels (380–450 ppm Ca), stable magnesium (1,250–1,350 ppm Mg), and alkalinity (8–12 dKH) is generally advised. Drops in either calcium or alkalinity can stall growth and cause segment whitening beyond the normal shipping-induced pallor.

LIGHTING: High-intensity lighting is generally advised; calcareous Halimeda species have been documented to require ample photosynthetically active radiation to support both photosynthesis and active calcification. PAR values in the 150+ µmol/m²/s range at the placement point are commonly recommended in the hobby.

GROWTH RATE: Growth tends to be slow relative to non-calcareous macroalgae, reflecting the metabolic cost of CaCO3 deposition into each new segment. Slow growth is normal behavior and should not be misinterpreted as decline.

SHIPPING WHITENING: Halimeda commonly arrives appearing white or pale following shipment, as chloroplasts migrate deeper into tissue in darkness. Coloration tends to return within a few hours of light exposure — this is well-documented behavior in the genus and is not indicative of poor health.

GRAZING RESISTANCE: The calcified segments are generally considered unpalatable to most marine fish and invertebrates, making this species relatively grazing-resistant compared to non-calcareous macroalgae. Urchins and some larger herbivorous fish have been reported to consume Halimeda segments in aquaria.

SOURCE COVERAGE CAVEAT: This entry is based primarily on the Mosaic Macros product page (sole known hobby-trade source at time of writing, 2026-06-08), supplemented by genus-level care data from sibling Halimeda entries (cactus-halimeda H. monile, carpeting-halimeda H. opuntia, coin-halimeda H. discoidea) and Wikipedia genus-level data. H. incrassata-specific sourcing was limited; water parameters and husbandry values reflect standard reef chemistry recommendations applied consistently across the Halimeda slice. Aquarists seeking species-specific data should consult AlgaeBase (taxonomy) and Caribbean reef ecology literature.

SIBLING SPECIES: See also cactus-halimeda (H. monile, cylindrical stacked segments), carpeting-halimeda (H. opuntia, flat segments creeping across rockwork), and coin-halimeda (H. discoidea, large rounded coin-shaped segments).
breedingNotes: Propagation in captivity is typically achieved by fragmentation — detaching a healthy segment chain and wedging it into sandy substrate or among rockwork until it establishes a new holdfast. Sexual reproduction (alternating gametophyte–sporophyte lifecycle typical of green algae) has not been documented in hobby aquaria. The genus Halimeda is known to complete rapid sexual reproduction in nature (~36 hours), but this is not a practical propagation method in captive systems.

primary.name: Mosaic Macros
primary.notes: Sole hobby-trade source found for this specialty entry. Product listing for 'Forked Halimeda' (Halimeda incrassata). Confirms scientific name, heavily calcified upright-from-substrate growth habit, low palatability to most fish, and shipping whitening behavior. Product image shows forked trilobate segment structure.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Halimeda (genus)
additional.notes: Taxonomy confirmed: family Halimedaceae, order Bryopsidales, class Ulvophyceae. Genus-level CaCO3 deposition data (segments 60–80% aragonite dry mass). H. incrassata listed as a species in the genus; no species-specific care data present. Distribution noted as tropical globally. H. incrassata specifically is documented as a sand-grower-holdfast species (lagoons, backreefs) per genus growth-form taxonomy. Wikipedia was inaccessible for the species-specific H. incrassata article (404); genus article used as fallback per playbook.

additional.name: Sibling Halimeda entries (cactus-halimeda, carpeting-halimeda, coin-halimeda)
additional.notes: Cross-referenced already-researched sibling species files (H. monile, H. opuntia, H. discoidea) to ensure consistent water parameters, macroalgae variant block values, and husbandry guidance across the Halimeda slice. Parameters align with genus-level reef husbandry standards confirmed across all three siblings.
